"This Bud's for You...")* (1984) * *[Bud Shank Quartet at Jazz Alley](/wiki/Bud_Shank_Quartet_at_Jazz_Alley "Bud Shank Quartet at Jazz Alley")* (1986) * *[California Concert](/wiki/California_Concert "California Concert")* (1985) * *[Serious Swingers](/wiki/Serious_Swingers "Serious Swingers")* (1986) * *[That Old Feeling](/wiki/That_Old_Feeling_(Bud_Shank_album) "That Old Feeling (Bud Shank album)")* (1986) * *[Quiet Fire](/wiki/Quiet_Fire_(Frank_Morgan_and_Bud_Shank_album) "Quiet Fire (Frank Morgan and Bud Shank album)")* (1987)

"**Bud**" **Shank Jr.** (May 27, 1926 – April 2, 2009)[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) was an American alto saxophonist and flautist. He rose to prominence in the early 1950s playing lead alto and flute in [Stan Kenton](/wiki/Stan_Kenton "Stan Kenton")'s Innovations in Modern Music Orchestra and throughout the decade worked in various small jazz combos. He spent the 1960s as a first-call studio musician in Hollywood. In the 1970s and 1980s, he performed regularly with [the L. A. Four](/wiki/The_L.A._Four_(band) "The L.A. Four (band)"). Shank ultimately abandoned the flute to focus exclusively on playing jazz on the alto saxophone. He also recorded on tenor and baritone sax. His most famous recording is probably the version of "[Harlem Nocturne](/wiki/Harlem_Nocturne "Harlem Nocturne")" used as the theme song in [*Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer*](/wiki/Mickey_Spillane%27s_Mike_Hammer_(1984_TV_series) "Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer (1984 TV series)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is also known for the soundtrack recordings with his group to the surfing films of [Bruce Brown](/wiki/Bruce_Brown_(director) "Bruce Brown (director)") in the late 1950s and early 1960s, and for the [alto flute](/wiki/Alto_flute "Alto flute") solo on the song "[California Dreamin'](/wiki/California_Dreamin%27 "California Dreamin'")" recorded by [the Mamas & the Papas](/wiki/The_Mamas_%26_the_Papas "The Mamas & the Papas") in 1965.
["Memoirs of a Famous Composer"](https://thescl.com/articles/memoirs-of-a-famous-composer/). *Thescl.com*. October 29, 2009. Retrieved August 1, 2021.

Weber, Bruce (April 7, 2009). ["Bud Shank, Jazz Saxophonist, Is Dead at 82"](https://www.nytimes.com/2009/04/07/arts/music/07shank.html). *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*. Retrieved April 7, 2009.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American saxophonist and flautist (1926–2009)

| Bud Shank | |
| --- | --- |
| [Bud Shank in 2006](/wiki/File:Bud_Shank.jpg "Bud Shank in 2006") Bud Shank in 2006 | |
| Background information | |
| Born | Clifford Everett Shank Jr.  (1926-05-27)May 27, 1926  [Dayton, Ohio](/wiki/Dayton,_Ohio "Dayton, Ohio"), U.S. |
| Died | April 2, 2009(2009-04-02) (aged 82)  [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), U.S. |
| Genres | [Jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") |
| Occupation | Musician |

Musical artist

**Clifford Everett** "**Bud**" **Shank Jr.** (May 27, 1926 – April 2, 2009)[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) was an American alto saxophonist and flautist. He rose to prominence in the early 1950s playing lead alto and flute in [Stan Kenton](/wiki/Stan_Kenton "Stan Kenton")'s Innovations in Modern Music Orchestra and throughout the decade worked in various small jazz combos. He spent the 1960s as a first-call studio musician in Hollywood. In the 1970s and 1980s, he performed regularly with [the L. A. Four](/wiki/The_L.A._Four_(band) "The L.A. Four (band)"). Shank ultimately abandoned the flute to focus exclusively on playing jazz on the alto saxophone. He also recorded on tenor and baritone sax. His most famous recording is probably the version of "[Harlem Nocturne](/wiki/Harlem_Nocturne "Harlem Nocturne")" used as the theme song in [*Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer*](/wiki/Mickey_Spillane%27s_Mike_Hammer_(1984_TV_series) "Mickey Spillane's Mike Hammer (1984 TV series)").[[2]](#cite_note-2) He is also known for the soundtrack recordings with his group to the surfing films of [Bruce Brown](/wiki/Bruce_Brown_(director) "Bruce Brown (director)") in the late 1950s and early 1960s, and for the [alto flute](/wiki/Alto_flute "Alto flute") solo on the song "[California Dreamin'](/wiki/California_Dreamin%27 "California Dreamin'")" recorded by [the Mamas & the Papas](/wiki/The_Mamas_%26_the_Papas "The Mamas & the Papas") in 1965.

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bud_Shank&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Bud Shank was born in [Dayton, Ohio](/wiki/Dayton,_Ohio "Dayton, Ohio"), United States.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) He began playing the [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") in [Vandalia, Ohio](/wiki/Vandalia,_Ohio "Vandalia, Ohio"), but switched to saxophone before attending the [University of North Carolina](/wiki/University_of_North_Carolina_at_Chapel_Hill "University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill").[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) While at UNC, Shank was initiated into the [Pi Kappa Alpha](/wiki/Pi_Kappa_Alpha "Pi Kappa Alpha") fraternity. In 1946, he worked with [Charlie Barnet](/wiki/Charlie_Barnet "Charlie Barnet") before moving on to Kenton and the [West coast jazz](/wiki/West_coast_jazz "West coast jazz") scene.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) He also had a strong interest in what might now be termed [world music](/wiki/World_music "World music"), playing Brazilian-influenced jazz with Laurindo Almeida in 1953 and 1954.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) In 1958, he became the first American jazz musician to record in Italy, with an Italian jazz orchestra conducted by [Ezio Leoni](/wiki/Ezio_Leoni "Ezio Leoni") (aka Len Mercer), paving the way for [Chet Baker](/wiki/Chet_Baker "Chet Baker") and others who would follow Shank's tracks recording in Milan with Maestro Leoni. In 1958 and 1960, Shank provided the soundtracks for two [Bruce Brown](/wiki/Bruce_Brown_(director) "Bruce Brown (director)") surf movies: *Slippery When Wet* and *Barefoot Adventure*. His world music collaborations continued in 1962, fusing jazz with Indian traditions in collaboration with Indian composer and sitar player [Ravi Shankar](/wiki/Ravi_Shankar "Ravi Shankar").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In 1974, Shank joined with [Ray Brown](/wiki/Ray_Brown_(musician) "Ray Brown (musician)"), [Shelly Manne](/wiki/Shelly_Manne "Shelly Manne") (replaced by [Jeff Hamilton](/wiki/Jeff_Hamilton_(drummer) "Jeff Hamilton (drummer)") after 1977), and [Laurindo Almeida](/wiki/Laurindo_Almeida "Laurindo Almeida") to form the group [the L.A. Four](/wiki/The_L.A._Four_(band) "The L.A. Four (band)"), who recorded and toured extensively through 1982.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1) Shank helped to popularize both Latin-flavored and chamber jazz music, and as a musician's musician also performed with orchestras as diverse as the [Royal Philharmonic](/wiki/Royal_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Royal Philharmonic Orchestra"), the New American Orchestra, the [Gerald Wilson](/wiki/Gerald_Wilson "Gerald Wilson") Big Band, [Stan Kenton](/wiki/Stan_Kenton "Stan Kenton")'s Neophonic Orchestra, and [Duke Ellington](/wiki/Duke_Ellington "Duke Ellington").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

In 2005, he formed the Bud Shank Big Band in Los Angeles to celebrate the 40th anniversary of Stan Kenton's Neophonic Orchestra.[[1]](#cite_note-AMG-1)

A documentary film about Shank, *Bud Shank: Against the Tide (Portrait of a Jazz Legend)*, was produced and directed by Graham Carter and released by Jazzed Media as a DVD (with a companion CD) in 2008. The film has been awarded four indie film awards including an Aurora Awards Gold.

Shank died on April 2, 2009, of a [pulmonary embolism](/wiki/Pulmonary_embolism "Pulmonary embolism") at his home in [Tucson, Arizona](/wiki/Tucson,_Arizona "Tucson, Arizona"), one day after returning from [San Diego, California](/wiki/San_Diego,_California "San Diego, California"), where he was recording a new album.[[5]](#cite_note-LATObit-5)[[6]](#cite_note-NYTObit-6)
